audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a man telephoning a friend to find out about their local public library.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Test, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Susie, it's Paul here.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: How are you?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Enjoying your new job?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: You're working at the library, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, I started when the library reopened a month ago.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It's great.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: The woman says that the library reopened a month ago.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So month has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Susie, it's Paul here.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: How are you?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Enjoying your new job?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: You're working at the library, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, I started when the library reopened a month ago.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It's great.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Actually, Carol and I have been meaning to join for a while.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Oh, you should.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It doesn't cost anything, and the new library has all sorts of facilities.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It's not just a place where you borrow books.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: For instance, there's an area with comfortable seats where you can sit and read the magazines they have there.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Some people spend the whole morning there.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Hmm, wish I had that amount of time to spend.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, you must be pretty busy at present, with the children and everything.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: We are, yes, but we're hoping to get away this summer.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: We're thinking of going to Greece.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Well, we've got a much larger section of the library devoted to travel books now.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: So you should come and have a look.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: I can't remember if there's anything specifically on Greece, but I should think so.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Now Carol's organizing a project for the history class she teaches at school.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: It's about life in the town 100 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Do you have anything that might be useful?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, actually we've now got a new section with materials on the history of the town and surrounding region.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right, I'll tell her.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: You can't always find that sort of thing on the internet.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Now, in the old library, there used to be a separate room with reference books.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: It was a really nice quiet room.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, we've put those books in the main part of the library now, but we do have a room called the community room.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It can be hired out for meetings, but at other times, people can use it to study.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: I might use that.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: It's hard to find anywhere quiet at home sometimes.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I can't remember how old your son and daughter are.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: We've introduced a special section of fiction written specially for teenagers, but they might be a bit young for that.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, they would be.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Well, we do have lots of activities for younger children.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Yes, for example, we have a science club.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: At the next meeting, they're going to be doing experiments with stuff that everyone has in the kitchen.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Sugar and flour and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: They might be interested.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, and we have a competition for children called Reading Challenge.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: That doesn't begin until after the end of term.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: They have to read six books, and they get a certificate if they manage it.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: So that gives them something to do while they're on holiday,
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: instead of getting bored.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: That's the idea.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: And there's special activities for adults too.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: On Friday, we have a local author called Tanya Streep, who's going to be talking about her new novel.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It's called 'Catch the Mouse', and she based the story on a crime that actually took place here years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: We're not free on Friday, but I'll look out for the book.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Now, this probably isn't for you, but we do have IT support available for members.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: We get quite a few older people coming along, who are wanting to get up to speed with computer technology.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: It's on Tuesday mornings, they don't need to make an appointment or anything.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: They just turn up.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Well, my mother might be interested.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: I'll let her know.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: OK, and there's another service which you wouldn't expect from a library, which is a free medical check-up.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: The hospital arranges for someone to come along and measure the level of sugar in your blood, and they check cholesterol levels at the same time.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, but that's only for the over 60s, so you wouldn't qualify.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: OK, well I'll tell my mother, she might be interested.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: What other information?
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Well, we do have a little shop with things like wall charts and greetings cards, and also stamps, so you can post the card straight away, which is really useful.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Well, I'll bring the children round at the weekend, and we'll join.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Oh, one more thing, I'll be bringing the car.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Is there parking available?
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Yes, and it's free in the evening and at weekends.
audioscript.speaker: SUSIE

audioscript.text: Perfect.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: Well, thanks, Susie.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: See you soon.
audioscript.speaker: PAUL

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a training session for new employees at a large travel company.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 17.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In this session in your training day, we're going to look at some of the more specialized holidays we offer at BC Travel.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, the travel business is very competitive and it's important to be aware of how the market's changing and developing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In terms of age groups, the over 65s are an important market, and one that's increasing steadily year on year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The fewest holidays are taken by the 31 to 42 year olds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that figure shows no sign of rising.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The biggest market at present is still the youngest group, the 16 to 30s, but this group's also seen the biggest drop over the last few years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Whereas there's a noticeable growth in the number of holidays taken by the 55 to 64 year olds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As far as the 43 to 54 year olds are concerned, bookings there are steady, but I have to say we haven't seen the increase we expected.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One trend we're noticing with nearly all age groups is the growing popularity of holidays in which clients do some kind of specialized activity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I'm not talking here about adventure holidays where clients take part in high risk activities like white water rafting, just for the thrill of it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Activity holidays usually involve rather less high risk sports or things like art and music.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're not necessarily cheaper than ordinary holidays, often the opposite in fact, but they do often take place outside the main tourist centers, which gives an opportunity for clients to find out more about the local people and customs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And many say this is one of the most positive features of these holidays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Of course, they offer the chance to develop a new skill or talent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But clients often say that more than this, it's the chance to create lasting relationships with other like minded people, that's the main draw.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let me give you some examples of BC Travel activity holidays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our painting holidays take place in four different centers in France and Italy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they're very popular with clients of all abilities from beginners onwards.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've got an excellent team of artists to lead the classes, some of them have been with us from the start, and five additional ones will be joining us this year, so that we can offer a greater number of classes in each center.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As far as cooking holidays are concerned, I know a lot of agents offer holidays where clients cook recipes related to one particular country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Usually the one they're staying in, but we focus on dishes from a great many different ones.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Apart from that, you'll find the usual emphasis on good quality organic ingredients.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's more or less a given nowadays, and there are generally some meat free recipes included.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Photography holidays take place in a wide range of countries from Iceland to Vietnam, and clients have the opportunity to see some stunning scenery.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Groups are small, no more than eight, so clients can have one on one tuition during the holiday, and excursions are arranged with fully trained guides.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At the end of each holiday, an exhibition is held of the photographs taken, so that clients can see one another's work, and receive valuable feedback from the tutor.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the training session, you have some time to look at questions 18 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 18 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Finally, let me tell you about our fitness holidays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Ireland and Italy, we run one week general fitness classes for all ages and levels of fitness.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Clients start the course with a consultation with a trainer, and together they draw up an individual program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as improving general fitness, clients find that they end up losing much of the stress they've built up in their daily lives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Greece, we have a two week holiday for clients who want to do something about their weight.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This has all the features you'd expect like a personalized diet program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But one of its most popular features is that the exercise classes are all held on the beach.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People say it's far preferable to being in a gym.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Finally, we offer several holidays in Morocco.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One very popular one is the mountain biking holiday.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Bikes are provided, and there are different routes according to people's ability.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We offer one which is tailored to the needs of families, which is particularly popular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, so that's about all the time I have today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, thank you very much.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Whereas there's a noticeable growth in the number of holidays taken by the 55 to 64 year olds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As far as the 43 to 54 year olds are concerned, bookings there are steady, but I have to say we haven't seen the increase we expected.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One trend we're noticing with nearly all age groups is the growing popularity of holidays in which clients do some kind of specialized activity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I'm not talking here about adventure holidays where clients take part in high risk activities like white water rafting, just for the thrill of it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Activity holidays usually involve rather less high risk sports or things like art and music.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They're not necessarily cheaper than ordinary holidays, often the opposite in fact, but they do often take place outside the main tourist centers, which gives an opportunity for clients to find out more about the local people and customs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And many say this is one of the most positive features of these holidays.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Of course, they offer the chance to develop a new skill or talent.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But clients often say that more than this, it's the chance to create lasting relationships with other like minded people, that's the main draw.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let me give you some examples of BC Travel activity holidays.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Our painting holidays take place in four different centers in France and Italy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they're very popular with clients of all abilities from beginners onwards.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've got an excellent team of artists to lead the classes, some of them have been with us from the start, and five additional ones will be joining us this year, so that we can offer a greater number of classes in each center.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As far as cooking holidays are concerned, I know a lot of agents offer holidays where clients cook recipes related to one particular country.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Usually the one they're staying in, but we focus on dishes from a great many different ones.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Apart from that, you'll find the usual emphasis on good quality organic ingredients.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's more or less a given nowadays, and there are generally some meat free recipes included.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Photography holidays take place in a wide range of countries from Iceland to Vietnam, and clients have the opportunity to see some stunning scenery.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Groups are small, no more than eight, so clients can have one on one tuition during the holiday, and excursions are arranged with fully trained guides.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At the end of each holiday, an exhibition is held of the photographs taken, so that clients can see one another's work, and receive valuable feedback from the tutor.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the training session, you have some time to look at questions 18 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 18 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Finally, let me tell you about our fitness holidays.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Ireland and Italy, we run one week general fitness classes for all ages and levels of fitness.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Clients start the course with a consultation with a trainer, and together they draw up an individual program.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as improving general fitness, clients find that they end up losing much of the stress they've built up in their daily lives.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Greece, we have a two week holiday for clients who want to do something about their weight.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This has all the features you'd expect like a personalized diet program.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But one of its most popular features is that the exercise classes are all held on the beach.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: People say it's far preferable to being in a gym.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Finally, we offer several holidays in Morocco.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One very popular one is the mountain biking holiday.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Bikes are provided, and there are different routes according to people's ability.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We offer one which is tailored to the needs of families, which is particularly popular.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, so that's about all the time I have today.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, thank you very much.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO age groups are taking increasing numbers of holidays with BC Travel?
Which TWO are the main reasons given for the popularity of activity holidays?
options: Clients learn about a different culture.
Clients are excited by the risk involved.
Clients find them good value for money.

items.title: How does BC Travel plan to expand the painting holidays?
options: by adding to the number of locations
by increasing the range of levels

items.title: Why are BC Travel's cooking holidays unusual?
What does the speaker say about the photography holidays?
options: The tutors are also trained guides.
Advice is given on selling photographs.

items: Location
Main focus
Other comments
Ireland and Italy
general fitness
personally designed programme
also reduces <input>
Greece
<input> control
includes exercise on the beach
Morocco
mountain biking
wide variety of levels
one holiday that is specially designed for <input>
body.title: Fitness Holidays

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two tourism students called Natalie and Dave talking about the tourism case study they are doing.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Dave, I'm worried about our case study.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: I've done a bit of reading, but I'm not sure what's involved in actually writing a case study.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: I missed the lecture where Dr Baker talked us through it.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: OK, well, it's quite straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: We've got our focus.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: That's tourism at the Horton Castle site.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: And you said you had done some reading about it?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Yes, I found some articles and made notes of the main points.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Did you remember to keep a record of where you got the information from?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Sure, I know what a pain it is when you forget that.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: OK, so we can compare what we've read.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Then we have to decide on a particular problem or need at our site, and then think about who we're going to interview to get more information.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: OK, so who'd that be?
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: The people who work there, and presumably some of the tourists too.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Yes, both those groups.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: So we'll have to go to the site to do that, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: But we might also do some of our interviewing away from the site.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: We could even contact some people here in the city.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Like administrators involved in overseeing tourism.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: OK, so we'll need to think about our interview questions and fix times and places for the meetings.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: It's all going to take a lot of time.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. And if we can, we should ask our interviewees if they can bring along some numerical data that we can add to support our findings.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: And photographs?
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: I think we have plenty of those already, but Dr Baker also said we have to establish with our interviewees whether we can identify them in our case study.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Or whether they want to be anonymous.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Oh, I wouldn't have thought of that.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: OK, once we've got all this information, I suppose we have to analyze it.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Yes, put it all together, and choose what's relevant to the problem we're focusing on.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: And analyze that carefully to find out if we can identify any trends or regularities there.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: That's the main thing at this stage, rather than concentrating on details or lots of facts.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: OK, and then once we've analyzed that, what next?
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Well, then we need to think about what we do with the data we've selected to make it as clear as possible to our readers.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Things like graphs or tables or charts.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Then the case study itself is mostly quite standard.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: We begin by presenting the problem, and giving some background, then go through the main sections.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: But the thing that surprised me is that in a normal report, we'd end with some suggestions to deal with the problem or need we identified.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: But in a case study, we end up with a question or a series of questions to our readers, and they decide what ought to be done.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Oh, I hadn't realized that.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So basically, the problem we're addressing in our case study of the Horton Castle site is why so few tourists are visiting it.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: And we'll find out more from our interviews, but I did find one report on the internet that suggested that one reason might be because as far as transport goes, access is difficult.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: I read that too, but that report was actually written 10 years ago, when the road there was really bad.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: But that's been improved now, and I think there's plenty of fascinating stuff there for a really good day out, but you'd never realize it from the Castle website.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Maybe that's the problem.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Yes, it's really dry and boring.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: I read somewhere a suggestion that what the castle needs is a visitor center.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: So we could have a look for some information about that on the internet.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: What would we need to know?
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Well, who'd use it for a start?
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: It'd be good to know what categories the visitors fall into too, like school parties or retired people.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: But I think we'd have to talk to staff to get that information.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: OK, and as we're thinking of suggesting a visitor center, we'd also have to look at potential problems.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: I mean obviously, it wouldn't be cheap to set up.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: No, but it could be a really good investment.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: And as it's on a historical site, it'd need to get special planning permission, I expect.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: That might be hard.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Especially as the only possible place for it would be at the entrance, and that's right in front of the castle.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: But it could be a good thing for the town of Horton, at present, it's a bit of a ghost town.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Once they've left school and got any skills or qualifications, the young people all get out as fast as they can to get jobs in the city, and the only people left are children and those who've retired.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Something else we could investigate would be the potential damage that tourists might cause to the castle site.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: I mean their environmental impact.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: At present the tourists can just wander around wherever they want.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: But if numbers increase, there might have to be some restrictions, like sticking to marked ways, and there'd need to be guides and wardens around to make sure these are enforced.
audioscript.speaker: NATALIE

audioscript.text: Yes, we could look at that too.
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: OK, well we certainly...
audioscript.speaker: DAVE

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: Locate and read relevant articles, noting key information and also <input>.
Identify a problem or need
Select interviewees - these may be site <input>, visitors or city <input>.
Prepare and carry out interviews. If possible, collect statistics.
Check whether <input> of interviewees can be used

Decide on the best form of visuals

items.title: WRITING THE CASE STUDY

body.title: STAGES IN DOING A TOURISM CASE STUDY

items.title: Natalie and Dave agree one reason why so few people visit Horton Castle is that
options: it is difficult to get to.
there is little there of interest.

items.title: Natalie and Dave agree that the greatest problem with a visitor centre could be
options: finding a big enough space for it.

items.title: What does Dave say about conditions in the town of Horton?
options: There is a lot of unemployment.
There are few people of working age.
There are opportunities for skilled workers.

items.title: According to Natalie, one way to prevent damage to the castle site would be to
options: make visitors keep to the paths.

test_text.title: Effects of environmental change on birds

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a lecturer on an environmental studies course discussing how birds are affected by environmental change.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so we've been looking at how manmade changes in our environment can affect wildlife.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, I'll discuss a particular example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's take a look at mercury.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Mercury is one of the 120 or so elements.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That make up all matter, and it has the symbol Hg.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a shiny silvery substance, you may have seen it in old-fashioned thermometers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it's not used much for domestic purposes now, because it's highly toxic.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the problem is that the amount of mercury in the environment is increasing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main reason for this is the power plants used to produce electricity.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main source of energy that most of them use is still coal, and when it's burned, it releases mercury into the atmosphere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of this gets deposited into lakes and rivers, and if it's ingested by a fish, it's not excreted, it stays in the fish's body.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it enters the food chain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, it's been known for some time that birds which eat fish may be affected, but what wasn't known until quite recently is that those that eat insects can also be affected.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, a woman called Claire Varian-Ramos is doing some research on how this is affecting birds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And rather than looking at how many birds are actually killed by mercury poisoning, she's looking for more subtle sub-effects.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these may be to do with the behavior of the birds, or with the effect of mercury on the way their brain works, so whether it leads to problems with memory for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And she's particularly focusing on the effects of mercury on bird song.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, the process of song learning happens at a particular stage in the birds' development.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And what you may not know is that a young bird seems to acquire this skill by listening to the songs produced by its father, rather than by any other bird.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And Varian-Ramos has already found in her research that if young male birds are exposed to mercury, if they eat food contaminated with mercury,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: then the songs they produce aren't as complex as those produced by other birds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So quite low-level exposure to mercury is likely to have an impact on male birds in a natural situation, because it can mean that they're less attractive to female birds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And so it can affect their chances of reproduction.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, the way she's carrying out this research is worth thinking about.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: She's using a mixture of studies, using birds kept in laboratories, and studies carried out outdoors in the wild.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The lab-based studies have the advantage that you don't get all the variables you would in a natural setting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So the experimenter has a much higher level of control, and that means they can be more confident about their results in some ways.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And of course, they don't have to worry about going out and finding the birds in order to observe them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, what are the implications here for humans?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, because many birds are migratory, they may be transporting mercury far from contaminated sites, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's been found that ducks who'd been feeding at a contaminated site were later shot by hunters over 1,000 km away.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And presumably eaten, but these birds likely had mercury levels high enough to warrant concern for human consumption.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In addition, going back to song learning by birds, we saw that this may be affected by mercury contamination.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, we also know that in humans, mercury causes developmental delays in the acquisition of language, and in fact this process is very similar in the brain regions it involves.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And even the genes that are involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But mercury contamination has other important implications for humans as well.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's now known that an unborn child can be affected if the food eaten by its mother contains high levels of mercury, and these effects can be quite substantial.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the end, it comes down to whether more value is placed on human economic well-being or environmental well-being.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's true, there are new regulations for mercury emissions from power plants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But these will need billions of dollars to implement and increase costs for everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some argue that's too much to pay to protect wildlife.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But as we've seen, the issues go beyond that, and I think it's an issue we need to consider very carefully.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a lecturer on an environmental studies course discussing how birds are affected by environmental change.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: OK, so we've been looking at how manmade changes in our environment can affect wildlife.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, I'll discuss a particular example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Let's take a look at mercury.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Mercury is one of the 120 or so elements.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That make up all matter, and it has the symbol Hg.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's a shiny silvery substance, you may have seen it in old-fashioned thermometers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it's not used much for domestic purposes now, because it's highly toxic.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But the problem is that the amount of mercury in the environment is increasing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The main reason for this is the power plants used to produce electricity.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The main source of energy that most of them use is still coal, and when it's burned, it releases mercury into the atmosphere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some of this gets deposited into lakes and rivers, and if it's ingested by a fish, it's not excreted, it stays in the fish's body.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it enters the food chain.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, it's been known for some time that birds which eat fish may be affected, but what wasn't known until quite recently is that those that eat insects can also be affected.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, a woman called Claire Varian-Ramos is doing some research on how this is affecting birds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And rather than looking at how many birds are actually killed by mercury poisoning, she's looking for more subtle sub-effects.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And these may be to do with the behavior of the birds, or with the effect of mercury on the way their brain works, so whether it leads to problems with memory for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And she's particularly focusing on the effects of mercury on bird song.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, the process of song learning happens at a particular stage in the birds' development.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And what you may not know is that a young bird seems to acquire this skill by listening to the songs produced by its father, rather than by any other bird.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And Varian-Ramos has already found in her research that if young male birds are exposed to mercury, if they eat food contaminated with mercury,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: then the songs they produce aren't as complex as those produced by other birds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So quite low-level exposure to mercury is likely to have an impact on male birds in a natural situation, because it can mean that they're less attractive to female birds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And so it can affect their chances of reproduction.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, the way she's carrying out this research is worth thinking about.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: She's using a mixture of studies, using birds kept in laboratories, and studies carried out outdoors in the wild.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The lab-based studies have the advantage that you don't get all the variables you would in a natural setting.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So the experimenter has a much higher level of control, and that means they can be more confident about their results in some ways.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And of course, they don't have to worry about going out and finding the birds in order to observe them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, what are the implications here for humans?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, because many birds are migratory, they may be transporting mercury far from contaminated sites, for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's been found that ducks who'd been feeding at a contaminated site were later shot by hunters over 1,000 km away.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And presumably eaten, but these birds likely had mercury levels high enough to warrant concern for human consumption.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In addition, going back to song learning by birds, we saw that this may be affected by mercury contamination.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, we also know that in humans, mercury causes developmental delays in the acquisition of language, and in fact this process is very similar in the brain regions it involves.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And even the genes that are involved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But mercury contamination has other important implications for humans as well.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's now known that an unborn child can be affected if the food eaten by its mother contains high levels of mercury, and these effects can be quite substantial.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the end, it comes down to whether more value is placed on human economic well-being or environmental well-being.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's true, there are new regulations for mercury emissions from power plants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But these will need billions of dollars to implement and increase costs for everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some argue that's too much to pay to protect wildlife.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But as we've seen, the issues go beyond that, and I think it's an issue we need to consider very carefully.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
